text.title: design-doc-advanced-patterns Core Rules
text.summary: Migrated testable rules for design-doc-advanced-patterns. Verify and refine before relying on for production.

rule.statement: Document component hierarchy as a tree (no ASCII art diagrams). Include a TypeScript interface definition for each component's props.
rule.rationale: Props interfaces become the source of truth. Implementation copies from design, not the reverse. Missing interfaces lead to inconsistent prop shapes across components.

rule.statement: Skip this section entirely for pure backend features.
rule.rationale: Component hierarchy adds no value when there is no UI to design.

rule.statement: List every new package and external service with: name, version (pinned range), purpose, license, and whether a fallback exists for local development.
rule.rationale: Undocumented dependencies are discovered at build time or in production. License tracking is a legal requirement for enterprise deployments.

rule.statement: Include a security table with columns: Concern | Mitigation | AD Reference. Every mitigation must reference a specific AD-X architectural decision. Cover at minimum: password storage, XSS, CSRF, SQL injection, and rate limiting.
rule.rationale: Security mitigations without AD references are not traceable. Code reviewers cannot verify that the mitigation was actually implemented as designed.

rule.statement: Use only NFR-X security values from spec.md for targets (e.g., bcrypt cost 12). Never invent security parameters.
rule.rationale: Agents default to generic use bcrypt without cost factor. The cost factor is a tunable performance/security tradeoff that must be specified in the spec.

rule.statement: For each performance concern: specify the concern, strategy, target (taken directly from NFR-X in spec.md), and AD reference. Never invent target numbers. If NFR is absent, write TBD, NFR required.
rule.rationale: Agents invent latency targets when spec NFRs are absent. Invented targets create false confidence and cannot be used as acceptance criteria.

rule.statement: Define three test layers: unit (coverage % target), integration (list all API endpoints to test with happy path and error path), E2E (list critical user flows, not just happy paths). Coverage targets must be project constants from constitution.md.
rule.rationale: E2E suites that cover only happy paths miss the highest-impact failure modes. Integration tests with only happy paths leave error handling unverified.

rule.statement: List specific test file paths for at least the unit layer. Agents cannot generate tests without knowing where they go.
rule.rationale: Vague write tests instructions produce tests in arbitrary locations that break CI path configuration.

rule.statement: Include a migration section only when design.md lists schema changes (new tables, altered columns, index changes). If no schema changes exist, explicitly state No migration required.
rule.rationale: Agents frequently generate boilerplate migration sections even when no schema changes exist. Explicit not applicable prevents confusion.

rule.statement: Migration strategy must include: rollout plan (staged percentages or feature flag), rollback procedure (step-by-step), and backward compatibility statement.
rule.rationale: Rollout without rollback is a deployment risk. Backward compatibility statement prevents breaking changes from going unreviewed.
